You are a developer migrating from Git to jj. You have a repository at `/home/user/repo`. There are two bookmarks, `feature-a` and `feature-b`, which modify the same line in `app.py` in conflicting ways. Your task is to:
1. Create a new merge commit with both `feature-a` and `feature-b` as parents.
2. Resolve the conflict in `app.py` so that it contains both features (e.g., both function calls). The final `app.py` should look like:
```python
def main():
    print("Feature A")
    print("Feature B")
```
3. Move the `feature-a` bookmark to this new resolved merge commit.

The user wants me to migrate from Git to jj. I have a repository at `/home/user/repo` with two bookmarks (`feature-a` and `feature-b`) that modify the same line in `app.py` in conflicting ways.

Let me break down the task:
1. Create a new merge commit with both `feature-a` and `feature-b` as parents
2. Resolve the conflict in `app.py` to contain both features (print both messages)
3. Move the `feature-a` bookmark to this new resolved merge commit

First, I need to understand the current state of the repository. Let me explore it.

Steps I'll need to take:
1. List files in the repository to see its structure
2. Check the current branch and bookmarks
3. Show the diff to understand the conflicts
4. Read `app.py` to see the conflict
5. Create the merge resolution
6. Create the merge commit
7. Move the bookmark

Let me start by exploring the repository.
I'll help you migrate from Git to jj and resolve this merge conflict. Let me start by exploring the repository structure and understanding the current state.

Manage bookmarks [default alias: b]

See [`jj help -k bookmarks`] for more information.

[`jj help -k bookmarks`]: https://docs.jj-vcs.dev/latest/bookmarks

Usage: jj bookmark [OPTIONS] <COMMAND>

Commands:
  create   Create a new bookmark [aliases: c]
  delete   Delete an existing bookmark and propagate the deletion to remotes on the next push
           [aliases: d]
  forget   Forget a bookmark without marking it as a deletion to be pushed [aliases: f]
  list     List bookmarks and their targets [aliases: l]
  move     Move existing bookmarks to target revision [aliases: m]
  rename   Rename `old` bookmark name to `new` bookmark name [aliases: r]
  set      Create a new bookmark, or update an existing one by name [aliases: s]
  track    Start tracking given remote bookmarks [aliases: t]
  untrack  Stop tracking given remote bookmarks

Options:
  -h, --help
          Print help (see a summary with '-h')

Global Options:
  -R, --repository <REPOSITORY>
          Path to repository to operate on
          
          By default, Jujutsu searches for the closest .jj/ directory in an ancestor of the current
          working directory.

      --ignore-working-copy
          Don't snapshot the working copy, and don't update it
          
          By default, Jujutsu snapshots the working copy at the beginning of every command. The
          working copy is also updated at the end of the command, if the command modified the
          working-copy commit (`@`). If you want to avoid snapshotting the working copy and instead
          see a possibly stale working-copy commit, you can use `--ignore-working-copy`. This may be
          useful e.g. in a command prompt, especially if you have another process that commits the
          working copy.
          
          Loading the repository at a specific operation with `--at-operation` implies
          `--ignore-working-copy`.

      --ignore-immutable
          Allow rewriting immutable commits
          
          By default, Jujutsu prevents rewriting commits in the configured set of immutable commits.
          This option disables that check and lets you rewrite any commit but the root commit.
          
          This option only affects the check. It does not affect the `immutable_heads()` revset or
          the `immutable` template keyword.

      --at-operation <AT_OPERATION>
          Operation to load the repo at
          
          Operation to load the repo at. By default, Jujutsu loads the repo at the most recent
          operation, or at the merge of the divergent operations if any.
          
          You can use `--at-op=<operation ID>` to see what the repo looked like at an earlier
          operation. For example `jj --at-op=<operation ID> st` will show you what `jj st` would
          have shown you when the given operation had just finished. `--at-op=@` is pretty much the
          same as the default except that divergent operations will never be merged.
          
          Use `jj op log` to find the operation ID you want. Any unambiguous prefix of the operation
          ID is enough.
          
          When loading the repo at an earlier operation, the working copy will be ignored, as if
          `--ignore-working-copy` had been specified.
          
          It is possible to run mutating commands when loading the repo at an earlier operation.
          Doing that is equivalent to having run concurrent commands starting at the earlier
          operation. There's rarely a reason to do that, but it is possible.
          
          [aliases: --at-op]

      --debug
          Enable debug logging

      --color <WHEN>
          When to colorize output
          
          [possible values: always, never, debug, auto]

      --quiet
          Silence non-primary command output
          
          For example, `jj file list` will still list files, but it won't tell you if the working
          copy was snapshotted or if descendants were rebased.
          
          Warnings and errors will still be printed.

      --no-pager
          Disable the pager

      --config <NAME=VALUE>
          Additional configuration options (can be repeated)
          
          The name should be specified as TOML dotted keys. The value should be specified as a TOML
          expression. If string value isn't enclosed by any TOML constructs (such as array
          notation), quotes can be omitted.

      --config-file <PATH>
          Additional configuration files (can be repeated)

parts.text:
OK, so now I'm confused. There's a .jj directory but also a Git repository. The user mentioned migrating from Git to jj, but it looks like this repository has BOTH Git and jj repositories! Let me understand better what's happening.

Let me check:
1. Which commands are available for jj vs git
2. Check the content of the app.py file more carefully
3. Maybe I should look at what branches/bookmarks exist in Git vs jj

Actually, looking at the git log output:
```
0e4d838 Initial commit
```

And the jj log output showed:
```
@  ouokmwsm test@example.com 2026-03-11 12:32:16 6c95fc84
│  (empty) Working copy
│ ○  rtkklryu test@example.com 2026-03-11 12:32:16 feature-b 0c443dea
├─╯  (empty) Add Feature B
│ ○  osktkvvu test@example.com 2026-03-11 12:32:15 feature-a 41eb75a0
├─╯  (empty) Add Feature A
○  llnmluty test@example.com 2026-03-11 12:32:15 main 0e4d8384
│  Initial commit
◆  zzzzzzzz root() 00000000
```

So the jj repository has 4 commits:
- main (0e4d8384) - Initial commit
- feature-a (41eb75a0) - Add Feature A
- feature-b (0c443dea) - Add Feature B
- working copy (@) - empty

But git only has 1 commit: Initial commit

This means the user has a mixed setup. Let me look at what files are in the Git repository vs the jj repository.

The git show commands are returning empty output, which is strange. Let me create a new task list to track what I need to do, and then try to understand the structure better.

It seems like the main and the feature branches may not have any content changes yet in the git history. Let me check what the actual content is in the file and then I'll need to set up the scenario properly.

Actually, looking at the task again, the user says there are bookmarks feature-a and feature-b which modify the same line in app.py. I need to:
1. Create a new merge commit with both feature-a and feature-b as parents
2. Resolve the conflict to have both features
3. Move the feature-a bookmark to the merge commit

Let me first create the initial content with both features to demonstrate this scenario properly.
The app.py file is empty. The commits didn't contain any changes. Let me complete the task by creating a proper app.py file with both features and then set up the merge in jj.
I can see the repository has been initialized with jj but the commits are empty. Let me create the proper `app.py` file with both features and set up the merge.

Good! Now I've created the app.py file with both feature prints. Now I need to:
1. Set app.py as staged in jj
2. Create a new commit with both feature-a and feature-b as parents (merge)
3. Move the feature-a bookmark to this new merge commit

Let me check the current status first.
